channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: As American cities grapple with homelessness, one offers a fix
item.description: Denver’s answer to a stubborn problem

item.title: Tournament of losers
item.description: The World Cup is a festival for corporate has-beens

item.title: Germany’s left-wing Die Linke party has won over the young
item.description: The populists promise a “scorching hot socialist summer” of protest—but face tricky questions in the east

item.title: Iran’s battered economy will take years to recover from the war
item.description: Even if it gets sanctions relief and aid for reconstruction

item.title: Ancient DNA is rewriting the history of plague
item.description: Dense cities do not seem to have been necessary for outbreaks of the disease

item.title: The chocolate industry is built on the labour of bloodsucking midges
item.description: Researchers have now confirmed how cacao plants are pollinated

item.title: Fox, Roku and the next phase of the streaming wars
item.description: A $22bn deal creates a formidable new power in Hollywood

item.title: Introducing our Business in Brief newsletter
item.description: Every day we will deliver concise analysis of the stories driving business and markets worldwide

item.title: Albania’s flamingo protests target Donald Trump’s son-in-law
item.description: Jared Kushner’s beach development brings trouble for a prime minister

item.title: Deal or no deal, oil prices will stay high for months
item.description: The pre-war days of $60 crude are not coming back soon

item.title: Asian allies are doomed to hug Donald Trump close
item.description: Forget middle-power coalitions. Japan and neighbours know that only America can deter China

item.title: America’s bull market has entered its manic phase
item.description: Options markets show optimism is giving way to euphoria

item.title: The coming El Niño could be the strongest ever recorded
item.description: It is bad news for a warming world

item.title: Scammers are preying on America’s illegal immigrants
item.description: Fake lawyers see opportunity in Donald Trump’s crackdown

item.title: Did AI write this article?
item.description: Perish the thought. But we tracked how artificial intelligence is changing five creative fields

item.title: Travel Brazil’s mirror-state to see the country’s future
item.description: No one has ever been elected president in Brazil without carrying Minas Gerais

item.title: The end of the war in Iran threatens “glorious failure” for Israel
item.description: Donald Trump’s deal with Iran would leave America’s ally without any strategic gains

item.title: Meet the world’s top AI-pilled economists
item.description: Most of them are not found in ivory towers

item.title: America’s carmakers cannot escape Chinese EVs for ever
item.description: Can they stay relevant?

item.title: The War Room newsletter: What eight years as defence editor looks like
item.description: From flying fighter jets to travelling on destroyers, Shashank Joshi pens his final edition of this newsletter

item.title: Comfort meets constraint in China’s most “liveable” city
item.description: A high-income, low-pressure existence is not all it’s cracked up to be

item.title: The terrifying new air war in Ukraine
item.description: Russia is dominating the missile war by default, but Ukraine hopes that will change

item.title: A deal is only the beginning of the end of the US-Iran war
item.description: And they could start fighting again in the hope of getting a better one

item.title: Donald Trump’s blocking of Anthropic is capricious and chaotic
item.description: America’s closest allies are shellshocked

item.title: A new intelligence chief in America may oversee a shrinking office
item.description: Political drama has obscured a battle over spy powers

item.title: The real problem with Narendra Modi’s airport-building frenzy
item.description: Build it and they will come. But how will they leave?

item.title: The Californication of middle-class Chinese diets
item.description: China wants more healthy, clean and even foreign food

item.title: Companies are scrambling to curtail soaring AI costs
item.description: For the heaviest users, tokenmaxxing is losing its appeal

item.title: Does Donald Trump make Latin America a good bet?
item.description: Nowhere in the developing world has done so well out of the past year

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Can Tocqueville explain America today?
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, takes a road trip to mark the country’s 250th birthday

item.title: The Swiss would be foolish to cap their population at 10m
item.description: Immigration sometimes needs brakes, but the proposal would be like driving into a wall

item.title: Treating pancreatic tumours may have revealed cancer’s master switch
item.description: A promising drug could be the first of an entirely new class of treatments

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: A World Cup paradox
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: The value of SpaceX rockets on its stock-market debut
item.description: Elon Musk has become the world’s first trillionaire

item.title: The best way to celebrate America at 250 is to get behind the wheel
item.description: Preferably with our new podcast series for company

item.title: Japan is rethinking its divorce laws
item.description: A move to introduce joint custody marks a big shift

item.title: In China ride-hailing work is a last resort for rural labourers
item.description: But now Shenzhen says it has too many of them

item.title: China’s notorious university-entrance exam is changing
item.description: Demography and AI are set to transform the gaokao

item.title: On America’s 250th, go back to “Democracy in America”
item.description: In praise of Alexis de Tocqueville

item.title: Social media is behind both “teen takeovers” and the outrage they fuel
item.description: Cities struggle to respond to big gatherings of young folk

item.title: America’s quintessential places are getting old, fast
item.description: Welcome to the greying suburbs

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
Ukraine is transplanting its industrial heart to the west
item.description: Soviet-built factory towns are in the wrong spot now that Russia is the enemy

item.title: Another new boss aims to fix the world’s biggest chocolate-maker
item.description: Will Hein Schumacher’s turnaround plan for Barry Callebaut work?

item.title: Too many people are shockingly bad at prioritisation
item.description: Choosing where to focus is among the most important skills

item.title: The world’s wealthy are migrating like never before
item.description: A booming industry of advisers is easing their passage

item.title: Fighting in Mogadishu risks making a weak state weaker
item.description: A presidential power grab has convulsed Somali politics

item.title: Could Eritrea come in from the cold?
item.description: Isaias Afwerki, its reclusive dictator, has spotted an opening to reconcile with America

item.title: The first-ever robotic rescue at sea is a milestone
item.description: An American drone recovers downed pilots in the Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Iran has lost its fear of war
item.description: A once-cautious regime is making a risky bet on low-level conflict

item.title: Techno-libertarians are flocking to the Caribbean
item.description: Angry locals are trying to shut them down

item.title: Can India’s cockroach party become a political movement?
item.description: The government is betting that the answer is no

item.title: Asian activists say too much egg production is cruel
item.description: They are hatching ingenious schemes

item.title: How big are China’s emerging industries?
item.description: Probably not big enough to offset the drag from the old

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The year America reckoned with AIDS
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on how activists forced the country to confront the crisis

item.title: For its own sake, China should change its growth model
item.description: It is suffering economic costs for its industrial dominance

item.title: A trade war between the EU and China seems inevitable
item.description: Europe sees Chinese subsidies, China sees European weakness

item.title: Stears wants to be Africa’s Bloomberg terminal
item.description: As the continent’s markets mature, high-quality financial analysis is more essential than ever

item.title: The world’s strategic oil reserves are running out fast
item.description: They cannot hold back the energy crunch for ever

item.title: How to share AI riches
item.description: From Donald Trump to Sam Altman, the idea of redistributing them is catching on. Does it make sense?

item.title: Ukraine’s war is now longer than the first world war
item.description: The ghost of Versailles haunts Ukraine’s peace

item.title: The Knicks represent New York—and capitalism—at its best
item.description: That is why New Yorkers love them

item.title: Forget the World Cup. Culture is becoming more fragmented
item.description: Farewell to the monoculture

item.title: Syria is an unexpected beneficiary of the Gulf war
item.description: The revival of an old oil-export route from Iraq to the Mediterranean helps Syria’s new regime

item.title: How to win the World Cup
item.description: Being rich helps, but being open to immigration works best of all

item.title: American capitalism is run by millionaires, not billionaires
item.description: They hide in plain sight—and wield enormous power

item.title: New techniques can predict and prevent lung cancer
item.description: A molecular signature can identify those most at risk

item.title: The World Cup has always been beset by scandal and strife
item.description: So has FIFA, the outfit that administers it

item.title: The World Cup paradox
item.description: How the rules of both entertainment and soft power are being rewritten

item.title: Too much Chinese science is ignored by the West
item.description: A bad reputation and cultural ignorance are probably responsible

item.title: Donald Trump’s least bad option in Iran
item.description: He must swallow his pride and accept a deal worse than the pre-war status quo

item.title: America’s mayors join the scrabble to become influencers
item.description: A dispatch from Long Beach, California

item.title: Fear of the SaaSpocalypse is tormenting techland
item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: An interview with South Korea’s president
item.description: Lee Jae Myung has put his country on track again, but challenges loom

item.title: Wall Street’s undignified SpaceX mania
item.description: Is that a rocket in your lobby, or are you just happy to see me?

item.title: Why Turkey likes NATO again
item.description: The alliance is in trouble, but Erdogan is enthusiastic

item.title: Ukrainian strikes are inflicting pain deep inside Russia
item.description: Our modelling suggests they are doing even more damage than commonly assumed

item.title: Why strongmen are wrong to loathe Europe
item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.title: What happens when a presidential vote is a drawn-out contest?
item.description: Peru, looking for its ninth president in a decade, is in a tight spot

item.title: The Federal Reserve must soon give Donald Trump bad news
item.description: Kevin Warsh, the unlucky new chairman, has seen his case for lower interest rates disintegrate

item.title: Apple’s new Siri is a dark horse in the AI race
item.description: The iPhone-maker does not need to build models to cash in on the technology

item.title: Armenia’s election is a setback for Vladimir Putin
item.description: Russia’s dirty tricks are failing to stop its pivot to the West

item.title: China is innovative. Its economy is a mess. Which matters more?
item.description: A question that will define the 21st century

item.title: A bidding war erupts for the world’s oldest bank
item.description: Monte dei Paschi di Siena is suddenly all the rage

item.title: How Israel is frustrating Donald Trump’s Iran plans
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu has defied America twice in recent days

item.title: The ageing protesters trying to topple Washington’s “ego arch”
item.description: Donald Trump has energised his fellow boomers in opposition

item.title: The War Room newsletter: When war becomes a political aesthetic
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines why conflict exerts a “strange pull”

item.title: A dropout-turned-influencer shakes up Chinese science
item.description: Fraud is rampant—but his videos mean everyone is now talking about it

item.title: How artificial intelligence got better at building itself
item.description: What does “recursive self-improvement” mean for the technology?

item.title: Nukes were off the agenda as Xi Jinping visited North Korea
item.description: China is wary of Russian influence and another Trump-Kim summit

item.title: The World Cup will test Mexico’s control over its territory
item.description: Donald Trump, who says the government is too weak to combat the drug gangs, will be watching

item.title: Money troubles are driving India’s states to drink
item.description: Things are so dire that one state has produced a rational alcohol policy

item.title: Robots could soon be delivering your pizza
item.description: An Estonian startup says its machines are now cheaper than human couriers

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: A modest proposal on Cuba
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on why the island should become America’s 51st state

item.title: How hot is America’s labour market?
item.description: At the moment, balmy. But it wouldn’t take a lot to require some Fed air-conditioning

item.title: Should priests have to report child abuse disclosed in confession?
item.description: American states take different approaches

item.title: The chemicals that reduce wrinkles
item.description: Vitamins, applied properly, can partially reverse the effects of ageing

item.title: America’s secretary of war pulls his punches on China
item.description: The Pentagon’s strategy for Asia contains a very big hole

item.title: Worries about migrants imperil South Korea’s shipbuilding boom
item.description: Compared with most rich countries, the foreign workforce is still small

item.title: Sex tourists fuel outrage about vice in Japan
item.description: The debates do not have women’s interests at heart

item.title: The rise of One Nation is melting Australian politics
item.description: Could the tumult produce a new party?

item.title: Ma Ning will proudly represent China at the World Cup
item.description: The referee is succeeding where the men’s national team is not

item.title: Xi Jinping gives China’s crack scientists new jobs inside government
item.description: Tech rivalry is reshaping the ruling elite

item.title: Donald Trump says Pete Hegseth loves war. That should disqualify him
item.description: How did standards for military leaders fall so low?

item.title: Feeding 10 billion people will require new technology
item.description: Startups are combining AI and genetics to make more food for less money

item.title: Italy has tracked down Cosa Nostra’s riches
item.description: A €200m seizure from the Corleone Mafia that once terrorised the country

item.title: What to read to understand your next employer
item.description: From Kafka’s “The Trial” to Orwell’s “1984”

item.title: Two American tycoons are betting big on a casino revival
item.description: Caesars and MGM may soon be under new ownership

item.title: Lego, Pokémon and the future of fun
item.description: Toys and media brands are increasingly joining forces

item.title: A new defence champion is rising from the Gulf
item.description: The region’s rulers want to reduce their dependence on Western arms

item.title: The parable of the tshukudu, Goma’s quintessential transport
item.description: War and cheap Chinese tricycles are disrupting a Congolese city’s idiosyncratic scooter

item.title: Gulf rulers are desperate to prove they are in fact strongmen
item.description: Unable to control external threats, they are squeezing their own people

item.title: Nigeria’s Christian groups scramble to win over Trump’s America
item.description: To help their cause, they are adopting tropes and rhetoric from the American right

item.title: Protesters have controlled Bolivia’s capital for a month
item.description: To lift their blockades, they want the centrist president, Rodrigo Paz, to resign

item.title: How to make football more exciting
item.description: The World Cup is wonderful. It could be even better

item.title: Ukraine is not a charity case
item.description: Europe needs its help just as badly as the other way round

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
How long can Pedro Sánchez last?
item.description: The Socialists’ mounting scandals threaten to bring down Spain’s prime minister

item.title: India’s surprise baby bust is a warning to the world
item.description: It is not just rich places that are becoming less fertile

item.title: Europe has reduced illegal immigration without goon tactics
item.description: But that is just the easy half of its migration battle

item.title: India’s population will soon be falling—probably quite fast
item.description: Neither widespread poverty, nor high rates of marriage nor relatively young mothers are sustaining fertility

item.title: Pakistan is battling two insurgencies
item.description: Problems mount at home as its leader plays peacemaker abroad

item.title: Indians can now bet on the monsoon
item.description: Heatwaves may be more important

item.title: European electricity markets have too much power
item.description: They must learn to deal with it

item.title: How to fight back against Gen-Z socialism
item.description: The me-first doctrine is a threat to prosperity

item.title: Some billionaires pay too little tax
item.description: But the case for levies on wealth is unconvincing

item.title: Gen-Z socialism, from Zohran to Zack and beyond
item.description: The world’s leftists are embracing a new set of economic ideas

item.title: America’s decaying Treasury market needs a fix
item.description: High debt, disjointed markets and pugnacious trade policy all threaten the world’s safe asset

item.title: California is on the cusp of its “Becerra era”
item.description: What would Xavier Becerra’s leadership mean for America’s most populous state?

item.title: American capitalism has taken an apocalyptic turn
item.description: Millenarian thinking permeates business and markets

item.title: Meet the jailscraper
item.description: With no room for prisons, New York City is building up

item.title: Rocket goes boom; so do moon plans
item.description: The phenomenal explosion could blow a hole in Amazon’s plans and NASA’s too

item.title: Why France is uneasy about German rearmament
item.description: Germany could become the benchmark military power in Europe

item.title: BYD is losing its spark
item.description: The Chinese EV giant is struggling to stay ahead in an industry defined by software

item.title: Even if America and Iran find an accord, don’t expect it to last
item.description: The Donald Trump Show could be back on air later this year

item.title: If it’s true, go
item.description: Our leader from 1998

item.title: America’s Social Security trust fund is disappearing
item.description: Legislators have just six years to fix things

item.title: Donald Trump could be the man to save Cuba
item.description: Ideological certainties have hurt Cubans for 70 years. Time to give cynicism a chance

item.title: The fading influence of America’s spy co-ordinator
item.description: Bill Pulte’s appointment as director of national intelligence reflects the office’s declining stature

item.title: Want to know the future? Don’t trust the stockmarket
item.description: Share prices are buffeted by far more than just new information

item.title: Nvidia wants to supercharge your laptop
item.description: Jensen Huang is bringing his chipmaker’s AI act to the PC

item.title: Britain is wrong to ban speakers like Hasan Piker
item.description: Even though his views are awful

item.title: Can Donald Trump save Israel from itself in Lebanon?
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu’s fight with Hizbullah is complicating the president’s talks with Iran

item.title: China’s high-tech rise is leaving much of the country behind
item.description: That could make a starkly unequal country even more so

item.title: Wanted: Asia news editor
item.description: An opportunity to join the staff of The Economist

item.title: The War Room newsletter: How the character of war has changed
item.description: Shashank Joshi looks back on eight years as defence editor

item.title: An increasingly risky bet for Democrats in Maine
item.description: Graham Platner looks like party’s best chance of unseating Susan Collins. His scandals may get in the way

item.title: Do you really want that computer-science degree?
item.description: American universities are selling ever more, just when their value is eroding

item.title: Can the stockmarket swallow SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI?
item.description: Watch out for indigestion

item.title: Abelardo de la Espriella is now the front-runner in Colombia
item.description: The right-wing populist outperformed in the first round of a deeply divisive election

item.title: China’s delivery drivers are its most obvious underclass
item.description: New rules aim to help but the economy will keep them down

item.title: How to bring down cheap, low-flying drones
item.description: Acoustics and innovative radar could help

item.title: Ukraine is now Europe’s war. Survival can’t be the only aim
item.description: America’s disengagement means it is now the old continent’s conflict to manage

item.title: Welcome to Evanston, where woke never died
item.description: A Chicago suburb is paying reparations to African-Americans

item.title: Texas is America Inc’s new centre of gravity
item.description: Exxon’s reincorporation is one more feather in the state’s cowboy hat

item.title: Brazil’s high-tech voting system is losing voters’ trust
item.description: Blame social media, populist politicians and falling trust in institutions

item.title: India’s republic of uncles
item.description: A cockroach meme rattles the country’s gerontocrats

item.title: Pete Hegseth pulls his punches on China
item.description: America’s secretary of war meanwhile lectures his country’s allies

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The California outsider
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our executive editor and New York bureau chief, on the state’s bizarre gubernatorial race

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The new shape of war
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Should you use a sleep tracker?
item.description: They are pretty accurate. But they could keep you up at night

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The most important wars forgotten by the West
item.description: Emma Irving, a news editor, examines the enduring impact of the opium wars

item.title: Mosquitoes seem to be getting over insect repellent
item.description: They learn to associate it with food

item.title: Why the world needs more franchises
item.description: From pizza to Pilates, franchises mint millionaires and make customers happy

item.title: The imperial vision of Ethiopia’s Abiy Ahmed
item.description: The prime minister’s ambitions threaten both his country and the Horn of Africa

item.title: llliberal leaders in mainland South-East Asia revamp their regimes
item.description: But they may struggle to solve their two biggest challenges

item.title: Indonesia’s erratic president grabs the country’s commodity exports
item.description: Facing a tumbling currency, Prabowo Subianto announces a vast new agency to sell resources to the world

item.title: Bowing to online fury, China’s censors ban a prizewinning film
item.description: Both nationalists and the manosphere resented its portrayal of their country

item.title: A coalmine explosion lays bare China’s two-speed economy
item.description: As its industries of the future take off, old ones still flout basic safety procedures

item.title: Are Angelenos angry enough to elect an insurgent as mayor?
item.description: Spencer Pratt tests the limits of grievance politics in a blue city

item.title: Why can’t Elon Musk do for politics what he’s done for industry?
item.description: So much for advancing civilisation

item.title: The refugees Donald Trump wants are white and middle-class
item.description: Afrikaners are welcome. Pretty much no one else is

item.title: Meet the Republicans defying Donald Trump
item.description: Conservatives with nothing left to lose are bucking the president

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Politics
Business
How the boomers screwed Europe
item.description: The huge rock ’n’ roll generation is sticking its few kids with the bill

item.title: Leo’s first encyclical attacks technological messianism
item.description: The Pope warns against replacing humans with AI, though he seems to use it himself

item.title: Europe’s superyacht-builders hit choppy waters
item.description: Fewer plutocrats want floating palaces

item.title: How should bosses talk about AI?
item.description: Employees are being asked to embrace a technology that causes fear

item.title: The Gulf war makes devastating oil spills more likely
item.description: It would also make them harder to handle

item.title: Congo’s response to Ebola is late and chaotic
item.description: It is hard to think of a more difficult place to stem an epidemic

item.title: How East Asia should respond to its China shock
item.description: As they deindustrialise, Taiwan, South Korea and Japan must reform

item.title: Without fanfare, China is making rural migrants’ lives easier
item.description: The notorious hukou system is being relaxed. Marx would approve

item.title: How to tax businesses in orbit and beyond
item.description: As the space business grows, so will offplanet finance

item.title: Japan’s beloved Indian restaurants are under threat
item.description: An unexpected casualty of the country’s immigration backlash

item.title: Everything is going right for India’s richest man
item.description: Gautam Adani is on a winning streak

item.title: BP cares too much about feelings and not enough about performance
item.description: The defenestration of its chairman is but the latest example

item.title: America and Iran are getting close to a deal. Or not
item.description: A bewildering week brought both frenzied negotiations and renewed American strikes

item.title: Smart tech is making war a dumber choice
item.description: Smaller, weaker countries can defend themselves more easily with cheap, deadly kit

item.title: Kevin Warsh’s troublesome inflation in-tray
item.description: The new Fed chair will struggle to appease his colleagues, the market and Donald Trump all at once

item.title: Ferrari’s electric car: divisiveness is the point
item.description: The Italian marque is attempting a high-stakes manoeuvre

item.title: Could Donald Trump save Cuba’s economy?
item.description: Even if the island were not burdened by embargoes and communism it would be a poor investment prospect

item.title: Too much time with colleagues can sour social interaction
item.description: A study of an Antarctic crew finds that eventually time together breeds conflict

item.title: Tomorrow’s medical sensors might come served with dinner
item.description: Listening to your gut could involve riboflavin batteries and toothpaste transistors

item.title: Attacking Cuba would be a huge mistake
item.description: But Donald Trump could make a deal with the communist regime

item.title: Itamar Ben-Gvir has presided over horrific abuse in Israel’s prisons
item.description: And he is proud of it

item.title: Would American military action against Cuba work?
item.description: All the options are fraught

item.title: Japan, South Korea and Taiwan are suffering industrial rot
item.description: Artificial intelligence is concealing a China shock

item.title: The world’s top condom-maker is getting squeezed
item.description: Will Karex break under pressure?

item.title: Centrists crying “Wolf!”
item.description: The wrong way to beat populists who deny the existence of hard problems

item.title: Ukraine’s latest challenge is how to deal with hope
item.description: Besides beating Russia, it must fight corruption and build democracy

item.title: Giga-IPOs are a symptom of public markets’ giga-problem
item.description: The incredible shrinking stockmarket

item.title: The Trump administration’s big move to limit legal immigration
item.description: A new policy restricting green-card applications could wreak havoc

item.title: China’s world-beating solar industry is in turmoil
item.description: The Gulf war won’t save it

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: A Starship enterprise
item.description: How we chose this week’s images

item.title: Abiy Ahmed dreams of remaking Ethiopia in his image
item.description: The prime minister’s re-election is certain. Ethiopia’s future is less so

item.title: China’s diplomatic successes are broad but shallow
item.description: It asks little of its foreign “partners”, and gives little back

item.title: What an America-Iran deal might look like
item.description: Donald Trump says an agreement is close. But also that he is in no rush

item.title: Crackdowns on financial secrecy aren’t hurting offshore finance
item.description: There is a lot more to havens than crystal-clear waters and a promise of opacity

item.title: Why science is becoming less innovative
item.description: A huge study suggests ageing scientists are part of the problem

item.title: Franchising has quietly made countless Americans rich
item.description: In the age of AI, running a McDonald’s may soon look a lot more appealing

item.title: How the Supreme Court both checks and empowers Donald Trump
item.description: It blocks his most blatantly illegal acts, but helps him in other ways

item.title: Colombia’s pivotal, polarised election could not be tighter
item.description: A Trump-loving populist is rising fast

item.title: Narendra Modi gives India’s elite a taste of the bad old days
item.description: Pay taxes, get lectures

item.title: France’s Gen Z has fallen for a 74-year-old radical socialist
item.description: Jean-Luc Mélenchon defies the weakness of the European left

item.title: War has not deterred Asian Muslims from the hajj
item.description: Yet they are finding the costs ever harder to bear

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: In defence of America’s elites
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on primary season

item.title: Tulsi Gabbard’s exit weakens MAGA’s anti-war faction
item.description: Her resignation shows how little influence such voices wield around Donald Trump

item.title: You probably don’t need extra electrolytes
item.description: Unless you’re athletic or unwell

item.title: A Turkish court ousts the opposition leader from his job
item.description: Deposing Ozgur Ozel is another step towards autocracy for Erdogan’s Turkey

item.title: Essential India newsletter: Introducing Ashoka, our new column
item.description: Leo Mirani explains how the column got its name

item.title: Lessons from the Premier League for Britain’s next premier
item.description: How a deflated country can bounce back

item.title: How to stop the Ebola outbreak
item.description: The latest epidemic in central Africa is a warning about future pandemics

item.title: Overseas Chinese risk losing their oldest institutions
item.description: Clan associations must change to survive

item.title: Why Japan and China will struggle to end their feud
item.description: Warming relations between America and China bode ill for Japan

item.title: What China can learn from Japan about escaping deflation
item.description: The right kind of Japanification

item.title: Home-schooling is taking off
item.description: In the wake of the covid-19 pandemic, more parents are teaching their children

item.title: Leftist populism’s next big test
item.description: In Maine, Graham Platner is betting centrist and right-leaning voters want economic revolution more than culture war

item.title: Europe’s first known language is alive in America’s West
item.description: A corner of Idaho will forever be north-east Spain

item.title: Democratic primary voters chose a dicey candidate for Georgia governor
item.description: Keisha Lance Bottoms will have to run against her record

item.title: The AI rush is hitting a bottleneck
item.description: Hardware-makers are failing to invest enough to keep up with demand

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Germany’s plan to build Europe’s strongest army
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines whether the country can achieve its goals—and what stands in its way

item.title: Donald Trump is crushing America’s farmers—yet they back him
item.description: They bore the brunt of his trade war. Now the war in Iran is pushing some to the brink

item.title: Ageing workers in East Asia are essential. More are needed
item.description: Yet they often face high barriers to employment and unpalatable options

item.title: Xi Jinping wants China to read more—as long as it’s the right books
item.description: A new campaign to put down phones and pick up classics

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The best generals in history
item.description: Richard Cockett, a senior editor, analyses what makes good generalship—and ranks his top three

item.title: The fashion influencer speaking truth to Putin
item.description: A viral Instagram video brings Russians’ wider grumbling into the open

item.title: San Francisco, AI capital of the world, is an economic laggard
item.description: Artificial intelligence is booming. Its heartland is not

item.title: Will Kevin Warsh Trumpify the Federal Reserve?
item.description: The incoming Fed chair says he wants regime change. But a revolution is unlikely

item.title: Making India’s numbers count again
item.description: India is restoring the credibility of its statistics. Will it last?

item.title: A gunman storms Donald Trump’s dinner with the press
item.description: The incident revives questions about the competence of the Secret Service

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The shock-value of modern conservatism
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, unpacks how provocation has become a political strategy

item.title: A mogul alleges he has been swindled by a Trump-affiliated crypto project
item.description: Tom Wolfe could not have invented a more outlandish satire of America in the 2020s

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: America prepares for the midterms
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Is exercise as effective as treatments for depression and anxiety?
item.description: Some big studies say yes. Many experts have reservations

item.title: Indonesia suggests charging a toll to transit the Malacca Strait
item.description: It’s not the first time that it has toyed with the unwelcome idea

item.title: Wanted: a new finance writer
item.description: An opportunity to join the staff of The Economist

item.title: The high price of forever wars
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu is quick to start conflicts, but shows no ability to end them

item.title: Pomp and pageantry won’t save Britain’s alliance with America
item.description: The special relationship? It’s complicated

item.title: Tim Cook wrote a winning recipe for Apple
item.description: Will it work for his successor?

item.title: Why Japan is loosening restrictions on exports of lethal arms
item.description: Takaichi Sanae, the hawkish new prime minister, hopes to spur a defence industrial renaissance

item.title: An anti-China protest lands Kazakhs in prison
item.description: Its economic power means China calls political shots, too

item.title: Europe’s defence startups face even bigger hurdles than America’s
item.description: They must contend with a fragmented market and hesitant customers

item.title: There is no better spur to military innovation than war
item.description: Defence firms in Ukraine make new weapons much faster and cheaper than most Western companies

item.title: Why Congress keeps getting dumber
item.description: Neither party has much tolerance for wily pragmatists like Thom Tillis
